abbreviations.PnETSpeciesParameters: Parameter than then calls a list of species.
abbreviations.FolN: Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.
abbreviations.SLWmax: Specific leaf weight at the top of the canopy.
abbreviations.SLWDel: Rate of change in the weight of the leaves from the top of the canopy (SLWmax) to each sublayer under the canopy.
abbreviations.MaxLAI: Maximum leaf area index (LAI) that a species can achieve in its lifetime (for an age cohort ?).
abbreviations.TOFol: Fraction of foliage biomass lost per year.
abbreviations.TORoot: Fraction of root biomass lost per year.
abbreviations.TOWood: Fraction of wood biomass lost per year.
abbreviations.AmaxA: Intercept of the relationship between foliar N and maximum net photosynthetic rate.
abbreviations.AmaxB: Slope of the relationship betwee foliar N and maximum net photosynthetic rate.
abbreviations.HalfSat: Half saturation light level for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.H1: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.H2: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.H3: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.H4: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.PsnAgeRed: Reduction factor of photosynthesis as the age of an age cohort increases.
abbreviations.LeafOnMinT: Minimum temperature for activate growing season.
abbreviations.PsnTMin: Minimum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.PsnTOpt: Optimal average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.PsnTMax: Maximum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.ColTol: Cold tolerance (coldest temperature that the species can survive).
abbreviations.k (parameter): Canopy light attenuation constant (light extinction coefficient).
abbreviations.DNSC: Proportion of Non Structural (ressources) carbon from the total activate biomass of the cohort that will be maintained as long as net photosynthesis exceeds maintenance respiration.
abbreviations.FracBelowG: Fraction of non-foliar biomass that is allocated belowground (root pool).
abbreviations.EstMoist: Scaling parameter that changes the maximum expected value of fWater when computing the probability of establishment.
abbreviations.EstRad: Scaling parameter that changes the maximum expected value of fRad when computing the probability of establishment.
abbreviations.FracFol: Fraction of the active woody biomass (above or belowground) of a cohort that determines the amount of foliage maintained each year.
abbreviations.FrActWd: Shape parameter of the negative exponential function that calculates the amount of woody biomass that has active xylem capable of supporting foliage.
abbreviations.CO2HalfSatEff: Slope coefficient used to reduce HalfSat (= increase shade tolerance) as CO2 concentration increase.
abbreviations.CO2AMaxBEff: Proportional change in AmaxB at 550ppm CO2 relative to 350ppm.
abbreviations.O3StomataSens: Species susceptibility of the stomata to ozone-induced sluggishness.
abbreviations.O3GrowthSens: Species susceptibility to ozone-induced tissue damage.
abbreviations.MaxFolN: Controls the dynamic response of foliar nitrogen (FolN) to light.
abbreviations.FolNShape: Controls the dynamic response of foliar nitrogen (FolN) to light.
abbreviations.MaxFracFol: Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.
abbreviations.FracFolShape: Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.
abbreviations.LifeForm: Specifies the life form (stature) of the species to determine the limits to the species major canopy layer.
abbreviations.MossScalar: Unit converter from biomass to depth in the case of a pseudo species (moss).
abbreviations.MaxCanopyLayer: Caps the number of canopy layers implemented in the model.
abbreviations.LayerThreshRatio: Define how age cohorts of a given species are grouped together into canopy layers. Correspond to the minimum ratio of cohort woody biomass density between two cohorts that will place them in different canopy layers.
abbreviations.PARunits: Indicates the units for PAR and HalfSat values. See Climate Input File for PAR and Species Parameters File for HalfSat.
abbreviations.IMAX: Defines the degree of subdivision into different canopy sublayers for each age cohort.
abbreviations.DVPD1: Coefficient for converting the vapor pressure deficit (VPD) into a reduction factor for photosynthesis caused by water pressure (fDVPD).
abbreviations.DVPD2: Coefficient for converting the vapor pressure deficit (VPD) into a reduction factor for photosynthesis caused by water pressure (fDVPD).
abbreviations.BFolResp: Define the Base Foliar Respiration Fraction (the foliar respiration as a fraction of the maximum photosynthetic rate).
abbreviations.MaintResp: Represents the loss of Non Structural Carbon (NSC) due to maintenance respiration as a fraction of the active wood biomass of the cohort.
abbreviations.Q10: Coefficient of the Q10 relationship to compute the foliar respiration.
abbreviations.FolLignin: Mass fraction of lignin in foliage tissue.
abbreviations.KWdLit: Annual decomposition rate of woody litter (decay constant k in equations).
abbreviations.InitialNSC: Amount of Non Structural Carbon assigned to newly established cohorts.
abbreviations.CFracBiomass: Carbon fraction of biomass by weight in an age cohort.
abbreviations.PrecipEvents: Number of precipitation events during a month.
abbreviations.PrecipEventsWithReplacement: Defines the logic of assignation of the precipitation from a precipitation event to the canopy sub layers.
abbreviations.ETExtCoeff: Canopy extinction coefficient to reduce soil water evaporation as LAI increases (because of shadow created by the canopy ?).
abbreviations.RETCropCoeff: Coefficient that scales the reference evapotranspiration (RET).
abbreviations.PreventEstablishment: Turns species establishment on new cells on or off.
abbreviations.Wythers: Turns the Wythers correction on or off.
abbreviations.DTEMP: Turns the PnET-II DTEMP temperature reduction factor on or off.
abbreviations.MaxPest: Tuning parameter to raise or lower the establishment probability of species by a same proportion.
abbreviations.AmaxFrac: Fraction of the early morning instantaneous photosynthetic rate that represents the mean daily photosynthetic rate.
abbreviations.InvertPest: Activates the optional inversion of the effect of fRad on the probability establishment as a function of HalfSat.
abbreviations.SoilIceDepth: Activates the optional simulation of soil ice depth and its effects on water leakage.
abbreviations.LeakageFrostDepth: Soil ice depth at which frozen soil no longer impedes soil drainage.
abbreviations.FrostFactor: Tuning parameter to adjust soil ice depth.
abbreviations.InitialCommunitiesSpinup: Specifies how the biomass of the initial age cohorts/communities in each cells are generated.
abbreviations.SpinupWaterStress: Activates waterlogging stress during spin up procedures.
abbreviations.Parallel: Specifies the number of CPU threads used for parallel execution.
abbreviations.CohortStacking: Specifies the canopy cohort processing method.
abbreviations.CanopySumScale: Scales the degree to which cumulativve canopy proportions cannot exceed 1.

children.label: Goal of this first calibration step

children.value: The goal of this first calibration phase, as recommended by

, is to calibrate the growth and mortality of a given tree species in “ideal” conditions. This means simulating a monoculture (no light competition with another species) on a mesic soil that retains water correctly (no drought, but no watterlogging either) and with a very mild, average historical climate (no extremes, especially in precipitations and temperatures). What I’ve attempted here is to automatize the advice and tips of
This first calibration phase is really the only one taking place in this current version of the notebook

, even though we might do some adjustments based on the results of the competition between the species (see Notebook 7). I had planned to calibrate the drought/waterlogging and temperature parameters, but have found it to be a quite complex endeavor for which we didn’t have the time yet. See
for indications of possible future improvements.

In this first step, we can isolate the effect of the many parameters and factor that influence tree growth, and focus on the few parameters that should (on a theoretical level) influence tree growth and mortality in ideal conditions :

There can be confusion between “optimal conditions in the study area” and “optimal conditions for the species”. The calibration tips of

recommands the later; but so as we have produced growth curves as close as the study area as possible (see notebook 4) to avoid confusing variants and genotypes, I’m going here with using “optimal conditions in the study area”. What that means is that we’re going to use precipitations and temperatures that are from the study area, but use their historical values that we will average to produce a “mild” climate with no extremes and very regular conditions (see Notebook 5). We will then make sure that the species match the peak of biomass (age and height of the peak), which we obtained for data near the study landscape (see Notebook 4) in these mild but local climate conditions.

To give you a broad idea of what we’re going to do here (which I finally started to understand after a lot of experimentation), we’re going to make sure that in the absence of as much reduction factor of photosynthesis as we can (fTemp for temperatures, fWater for drought/waterlogging, fAge for longevity, etc.), the productivity of species is enough so that it reaches the biomass peak found in the empirical data. We’re going to adjust this productivity so that not only do we match the peak height and timing (age), but we do so with the right amount of foliage/LAI (to make sure that species of different shade tolerance have the right amount of LAI; see Notebook 4 as to where we get these LAI targets), and by making sure that the beginning of the growth curve is acceptable. Finally, we calibrate the senescence using the methodology of Brian Sturtevant’s team.
All of this is a jungling act between many parameters, because changing one parameter (e.g.

children.title: Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.

children.value: for the overall productivity of the cohort) will often change different things in return (e.g. changing both the peak age and the peak height and the LAI). That why we will do it in different “substeps” where we try to focus on one aspect of the curve at the time.
I’ve composed the algorithms of each substeps myself (e.g. “First, change

children.title: Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.

children.value: until the peak height is right; then, change SLWMax to change the LAI; etc.”), and then used AI models to transform them into python functions (which are located in

). Their outputs can change a bit and not be completly uniform, but I made sure that each functions describes precisely what is going on (i.e. what parameter is being changed, what is the target, etc.).
Current limitations of this calibration methodology :

children.label: Current limitations of this calibration methodology :

children.title: Fraction of wood biomass lost per year.
Fraction of root biomass lost per year.

children.value: are used as kind of “garbage bins” to adjust the height of the biomass peak without changing the speed/age/timing of the peak too much by throwing away a part of the biomass of the cohort at each timestep

. It’s really hard to have empirical/realistic estimates of the values of these two parameter for real trees, and to be able to juggle all of the other parameters without dealing with a lot of interactions between their effects; because

children.title: Fraction of wood biomass lost per year.
Fraction of root biomass lost per year.

children.value: are quite predictable in their effect, their are used a lot to alter the biomass peak height. I’ve talked with Eric Gustafson, and he told me that it’s surely quite an inevitable part of the model (that we need to have some parameters that allow us to adjust the rest). I quite agree with him.
The algorithms can sometimes get stuck into the wrong places of the parameter space despite the checks I’ve implemented

. This is because all the algorithms are quite simple, and usually change one or two parameters at a time until reaching a target. It worked quite well for me so far; but if you’re getting strange results or strange outputs, you should read the outputs to see what’s going on, and most likely adjust your initial parameter values (or the criterias/targets of the functions). One of the most common thing that happen is that increasing

children.title: Fraction of the active woody biomass (above or belowground) of a cohort that determines the amount of foliage maintained each year.

children.value: doesn’t increase the LAI of the cohort because we’ve put it in the situation where the cohort is starved for carbon; you can detect that by seeing that the wood biomass of the cohort is very low.
You can imagine that the algorithms trying to find the right combination of parameters for your targets is a bit like playing golf

. Our goal is to reach a certain area of the parameter space, where the model gives us the behavior that we want. Moving the golf ball across the parameter space is done by changing the values of our parameters. But it’s like the golf course (the parameter space) contains hazards, holes, bunkers, water surfaces, or other things where our golf ball can get trapped.

The golf course as a metaphor for the PnET-Succession parameter space that we’ll be trying to traverse, filled with sand bunkers in which our ball can get trapped. While the algorithms used here try to avoid such traps as much as possible, there’s a good change that one of the algorithms will get stuck for at least one of your species.

Once we’re in such traps, it can be difficult or even impossible to get out by simply pushing our ball around as we’ve been doing so far. The algorithms I’ve designed here (with a lot of trial and error) are made to avoid these situations; but I’m certain that such situations will arise, especially because the parameter space of PnET-Succession is very big and complex. Also, keep in mind that sometimes, there simply isn’t any “end” to our golf course, because the target we’re trying to reach (or the combination of targets) is just impossible for the model to do with the given constraints (i.e. bounds on the parameters values, or simply the functioning of the model). In these cases, the algorithms are designed to return either a warning allowing you to manually change the parameters or reconsider your targets; or to return the best parameters that it found (closest to what you wanted).

One of the recurring problems is at the beginning of the growth of the cohort; sometimes, it’s quite difficult to make a cohort grow fast enough to reach its targeted biomass peak age, without making it grow too much, and vice-versa. When this happens, the algorithms are stretching the models into strange locations of the parameter spaces where you will start to see things like “bumps” in the LAI of the cohorts near its peak, etc. This means that we’re pushing into parameters that make the model behave quite unrealistically. If you’re getting stuck into these kind of situations, it might be good to double check your growth curves (Notebook 4) to be certain of what you’re asking the model to do. You might also try to change the generic parameters - like

children.title: Represents the loss of Non Structural Carbon (NSC) due to maintenance respiration as a fraction of the active wood biomass of the cohort.

children.value: -, but be warned that they will affect all the other species. Improvements of the algorithms (by editing the function used in the substep that is failing) is also possible.
Be certain of your weather data

Be certain to have good weather data. Because biomass accumulation is ultimately limited by water, your precipitation inputs should not be much greater than is typical for your study area, especially for the results of your simulation to match your empirical data better.
In this notebook, I’m using averaged climate data from 1950-2014 (see Notebook 5) as the climate data stream.

The values are averaged in order to provide a “mild” climate that represent ideal growth conditions for the species (as recommended by Eric Gustafson). We’re taking data from up to 1950 to represent growing conditions as similar as possible to those who gave rise to the growth data we have used in notebook 4.
Empirical growth curves are less important than the relative differences between species

, keep in mind that calibrating growth to strictly match empirical growth curves may cause competition behavior in the model that is unrealistic. As such, you may want to favor a shape that is somewhat consistent with other species that have the same growth strategy (e.g., pioneers v. shade-tolerants). This will be important for the next phase (calibrating competition outcomes).

the model equations may produce a curve shape that is somewhat different than your empirical curves (likely generated under specific conditions) and forcing the model to exactly match empirical curves may reduce its validity in landscape simulations. In particular, you should try to match the biomass accumulation where growth begins to plateau and produce relative rates of initial growth that reflect each species’ growth strategy (i.e., continuum of pioneer v. shade-tolerant)
The effect of longevity/age is disabled for the first substeps
For the first calibration substeps, you will see that the growth curves never go down, but will reach a plateau and stop.
This is because the first calibration substeps remove the effect of the longevity parameters (by putting the longevity of each species at 999 years), in order to remove the effect of age on growth.
The effect of age will be calibrated in section 1.4 with the calibration of senescence.
Code cells will display useful outputs

Each code cell will print detailed output about how the simulation is going on (evolution of the targets, parameter changes, etc.), allowing you to detect if one of the step is getting “stuck”, and why. When the calibration is finished for a species, the code will display a graph showing you the growth of the species wood biomass, LAI, and so on.
This substep is divided into two further sub-sub-steps, in order to follow the advice of

In a sense, this substep acts as a “sanity check” for the rest; if the growth of your species is wildly different than what’s expected, this step will help you detect it and diagnose it. This will prevent you from wasting time and getting the other algorithms stuck because your initial parameters were producing values that did not make sense.

Once the biomass peak is re-adjusted, the algorithm will check if the LAI has changed too much because of this, and will attempt to re-calibrate the LAI, and then re-calibrate the peak once again. There’s a limit of 3 iterations of this bigger loop to try to make the LAI and the biomass peak converge. If they don’t, an error message will display.
These two sub-sub-steps use the biomass peak height and LAI targets defined in Notebook 4.

## Calibration sub-sub-phase 1.1.1 : We change TORoot and TOWood to make large adjustments to
# the biomass peak, while keeping them in bounds. If we can't make larget adjusments to the biomass
# peak enough while staying in the bounds, we run diagnostics and display information to the user.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

# Modifying the FolN of some species who don't grow enough or grow too much with their
# initial parameters, which make it that they don't passe the first diagnostic test.
pnetSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
# ABIE.BAL : Grows a bit too much, reducing FolN a little
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["ABIE.BAL"]["FolN"] = "1.1"
# ACER.SAH : Doesn't grow enough, increasing FolN a little
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["ACER.SAH"]["FolN"] = "2.6"
# ACER.SAH : LAI really isn't right. Increasing FracFol a little
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["ACER.SAH"]["FracFol"] = "0.023"
# LARI.LAR : Grows too much, reducing FolN a litle
# Not too much ! Because it's deciduous, it still has to renew its leaves every year
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["LARI.LAR"]["FolN"] = "1.5"
# pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["LARI.LAR"]["FracFol"] = "0.022"
# Current SLWmax of LARI.LAR is really wrong compared to previous studies and LMA in TRY. Changing.
pnetSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]["LARI.LAR"]["SLWmax"] = "93.58"

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    if SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Evergreen"] == "Y":
        functionalType = "evergreen"
    else:
        functionalType = "deciduous"
        
    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_phase1_1(
        species             = speciesCode,
        functional_type     = functionalType,
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],      # g/m² — from NFI data
        LAI_min             = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"] - 1, # Arbitrary, based on my experience with the variation of the LAI
        LAI_target          = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        LAI_max             = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"] + 1, # Arbitrary, based on my experience with the variation of the LAI
        dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(
            open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = pnetSpeciesParameters,
        dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(
            open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
        DictOfBounds        = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json"))
    )

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = pnetSpeciesParameters
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    if resultDict[species] is None:
        raise ValueError("One of the initial diagnostics for one of your species (" + str(species) + ") has failed. Check the outputs of the cell to find out why, and modify the parameters until you can reach the right biomass peak with realistic values of TOWood and TORoot.")
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TOWood"] = resultDict[species]["TOWood"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TORoot"] = resultDict[species]["TORoot"]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

## Calibration sub-sub-phase 1.1.2 : We calibrate and stabilize the LAI
# of the cohort to be within a range around the maximum LAI proposed by Gustafon
# in the user guide, by tweaking FracFol, SLWMax and FrActWood.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    print("#" * 80)
    print(f"CALIBRATING THE LAI OF {speciesCode}")
    print("#" * 80)
    
    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_LAI_subphase_1_1_2(
        speciesCode,
        target_LAI_min=SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"] - 1,# Arbitrary, based on my experience with the variation of the LAI
        target_LAI_max=SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        DictOfBounds=json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        initial_core_params_path="./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json",
        initial_pnet_species_params_path="./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json",
        initial_generic_params_path="./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/InitialGenericParameters.json",
        duration=300,
        climate="mild",
        soil="SILO",
        n_bisection_iterations=20,
        verbose=True
    )

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.1/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FracFol"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["FracFol"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["SLWmax"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["SLWmax"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FrActWd"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["FrActWd"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TOWood"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["TOWood"]
    # dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TORoot"] = resultDict[species]["final_params"]["TORoot"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species] = resultDict[species]["updated_pnet_species_params"]["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

children.value: Sub-step 1.2 : Calibrating the biomass peak age

children.label: Sub-step 1.2 : Calibrating the biomass peak age

children.value: In this sub-step, we focus on calibrating the age at which the biomass peak of the cohort happens. This has a large influence on the beginning of the growth curve, which in turn will influence the competition between our species a lot.
The biomass peak age is derived from the empirical growth curves in Notebook 4.
This sub-step was the most complicated for which to create an algorithm, because of two reasons :

We are trying to calibrate the peak age, but still keep the right peak height (amount of biomass) and the right LAI. But altering one often alters the others, meaning with have to do regular corrections to the peak height and the LAI as we keep exploring the parameter space to get the right peak age.
It’s more difficult to move the peak age than it is to move the peak height and the LAI. There are four parameters we act on for this :

children.title: Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.
Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.
Shape parameter of the negative exponential function that calculates the amount of woody biomass that has active xylem capable of supporting foliage.
Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.

This is so that we don’t put ourselves in a problematic situation where the LAI and the peak height become so absurdly large (or small) that our cohort simply cannot grow correctly anymore, which then messes up the algorithm as it tries to keep adjusting FrAcWd. By constantly adjusting, we might take a bit more time, but we’re coming to it quite well in the end.

Sometimes, especially if your peak age is too low, the algorithm will not be able to reach it. In this kind of situations, it might be better to embrace something that is close to your empirical data, even if it’s not at your empirical data.

If the algorithm doesn’t work for your species, you might have to use the advice above to find the right parameter set manually, by exploring the parameter space by yourself.
About the tips of Eric Gustafson for the wood biomass at age 14

, Eric Gustafson recommands (by his own experience with the model) to make sure that the cohorts achieve a certain amount of wood biomass by their age 14, in order to distinguish the species in terms of their shade tolerance, to make sure that the more intolerant ones grow faster than the others :

However, I was never able to reach these values. My species tended to grow way too slow for this. More generally, my biomass targets (biomass peak from the growth curves, see Notebook 4) were much lower than previously published studies. My current interpretation is this : Eric (and other researchers in the US) tended to use sample plot data or growth curves from the Forest Vegetation Simulator (which is based on sample plot data) to make the growth curves that served as references for this calibration step. However, I use the NFI data, which reports much lower values of biomass (in g/m2) than what sample plot data or the FVS give. It’s possible that it’s because the sample plot and FVS curves contain a lot of “extremes” because of the variability inherant to the sample plot data, while the NFI data is much more “averaged”, but most likely much closer to the truth too.

There’s also the question of transforming the estimates of biomass from sample plots from biomass (g) to biomass per surface (g/m2). There are always a lot of different hypothesis contained in this translation. The NFI data is already in g/m2, but sample plots and FVS curves often require to use hypothesis/assumptions to translate to biomass per surface. Again, I tend to think that the NFI data is closer to the mark, especially because it’s closer to what LANDIS-II does (LANDIS-II only does biomass per surface).

children.value: If you calibrate the growth curve of your species using these parameters (and you really need to, because it’s very difficult to calibrate the initial growth of the species without them), you’re making sure that what you’re doing is going to act very differently as soon as you put your species in different conditions of shade. For example, this effect will completely disappear if your species implant in a pretty shaded pixel, or be too strong if it comes in completely open conditions, etc.

It will be good if the behavior of these parameters might be updated in the future, or if different parameters could be related to something more predictable and stable than fRad (for example, the age of the cohort directly); but that will be fore new versions of PnET-Succession. For now, I’m going with this. Just keep these limitations in mind, especially the fact that tree species that depend a lot on these parameters for their initial growth will be very suppressed if they grow in conditions with high shade.
This is the step most likely to fail
This calibration step is the most delicate, because we’re basically asking the model to grow a species at a certain speed, but also to reach a certain growth peak.

Some species will not manage this in the model, for different reasons : the climate that you have given is not good enough for them, or “too good”; the peak age and peak biomass are too big or too small relative to each other; and so on.
If this step fails, try to understand what’s going on, and check your growth curves. You can also relax the tolerance of the algorithm via additional parameters (see

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_subphase_1_2(
        species = speciesCode,
        target_peak_time = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_age"],   # years (from NFI data)
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        target_LAI = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        DictOfBounds = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        path_core = './SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json',
        path_pnet = './SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json',
        path_generic = './SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json',
        duration = 300,
        climate = "mild",
        soil = "SILO")

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["MaxFracFol"] = resultDict[species]["MaxFracFol"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FracFolShape"] = resultDict[species]["FracFolShape"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FrActWd"] = resultDict[species]["FrActWd"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FracFol"] = resultDict[species]["FracFol"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TOWood"] = resultDict[species]["TOWood"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["TORoot"] = resultDict[species]["TORoot"]
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FolN"] = resultDict[species]["FolN"]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

children.value: Sub-phase 1.3 (optional) : Accelerating early growth for species that grow too slowly

children.label: Sub-phase 1.3 (optional) : Accelerating early growth for species that grow too slowly

children.value: By calibrating the peak timing (sub-phase 2), it happens that some species do indeed get the right timing; but that they spent the first decades (sometimes up to 50 years) of their lives almost not growing at all.

This seems to be the result of an overall lack of productivity for these species in their early years which translate to a low LAI, and vice versa. This lack of productivity/foliage can be caused by different things at once, like the species having a low productivity to begin with (low

children.title: Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.

children.value: ), a combination of parameters that affects the LAI which make it difficult for the species to accumulate it in its early stages (

children.title: Fraction of the active woody biomass (above or belowground) of a cohort that determines the amount of foliage maintained each year.

children.value: The problem is that if we try to change these parameters, we also affect the rest of the growth of the species; especially its biomass peak timing and peak height.

I’ve tweaked a lot with different parameters to try to find something that would allow us to accelerate the early growth, while not moving the rest of the growth curve too much. It’s quite difficult, especially if we need to really increase the early growth of species. The best parameters I’ve found are

These parameters have been added to the PnET-Succession code by Eric Gustafson for this exact goal (influencing the early growth but not the rest of the curve as much as possible). So here, we increase these parameters to make the early growth faster; and because we will change the rest of the curve anyway, we correct the LAI with

children.title: Specific leaf weight at the top of the canopy.

children.value: again. It’s really a game of jungling at this point. Hopefully, this is the last complex step.

There remains the question of the target of early growth; how much do we want to increase the early growth of the species ? When do we stop ? I pondered about using another target, that we would call the “Biomass reached at 50% of the peak timing”. We could extract this value from the NFI data and GAMs in notebook 4. But after trying to deal with this, I realize that for the Black spruce (

, which had a slower growth than necessary and which was the motivation for this step) demanded too much adjustments to reach the right value of its biomass reached at 50% of the peak timing (as derived from NFI data). The model simply wouldn’t go there without creating other big issues and pushing the parameters to extra high values. I’ve got some hypothesis as to why this might be; it could be issues with the NFI data which overestimate early growth, or the fact that the parameters in the current PnET-Succession equation are too static and should change with the age of the cohort (i.e. the allocation of photosynthesis to the different part of the trees, or its turnover of wood and leaves, etc., are not the same when the tree is young versus when it gets old).
So, I went with a simple rule that worked well in the case of the Black spruce :
the biomass reached at 50% of the peak timing should be at least 30% of the biomass peak height
. That’s what this calibration step tries to ensure. For the rest, I leave my trust in the hands of the PnET model.
This step is simpler than others and might get stuck

The calibration algorithm used here is a bit simpler than the others because there are more parameters, and because I was running out of time. It might get stuck and overshoot its targets at some points. Be careful with it !
This step will be skipped for most species
If the early growth of the species is already good enough for a given species, this step will simply be skipped.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    # Using a peak timing tolerance of 7 years seems OK to me; it's arbitrary, and you can change it if needed.
    # If the tolerance is to low, this algorithm might have trouble reaching it though.
    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_subphase_1_3(
        species = speciesCode,
        target_peak_time = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_age"],   # years (from NFI data)
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        peak_time_tolerance = 7,
        target_LAI = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum LAI target"],
        dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
        DictOfBounds = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        duration = 300,
        climate = "mild",
        soil = "SILO")

# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.2/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

# Detect if MaxFolN/FolNShape have been used for one of the species
# If that's the case, we will provide values that disable their effect for other species who didn't need it,
# as all species need to have the parameter
MaxFolN_FolNShape_enabled = False
for species in resultDict:
    if "MaxFolN" in resultDict[species].keys() or "FolNShape" in resultDict[species].keys():
        MaxFolN_FolNShape_enabled = True
        break

if MaxFolN_FolNShape_enabled:
    for species in resultDict:
        if "MaxFolN" not in resultDict[species].keys() or "FolNShape" not in resultDict[species].keys() or resultDict[species]["MaxFolN"] == "Parameter is not in dictionnary" or resultDict[species]["FolNShape"] == "Parameter is not in dictionnary":
            resultDict[species]["MaxFolN"] = dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["FolN"]
            resultDict[species]["FolNShape"] = 1

for species in resultDict:
    for parameter in ["MaxFracFol", "FracFolShape", "FracFol", "MaxFolN", "FolNShape", "FolN", "TOWood", "TORoot"]:
        # if parameter in resultDict[species].keys() and resultDict[species][parameter] != "Parameter is not in dictionnary":
        if parameter in resultDict[species].keys():
            dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][parameter] = resultDict[species][parameter]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

children.title: Reduction factor of photosynthesis as the age of an age cohort increases.

children.value: Longevity (LANDIS-II Core species parameter; is outside of PnET Succession)

The last phase of the curve to calibrate is the senescence phase, where the tree cohort grows older. At it grows, its maintenance cost (in carbon) will grow; but its photosynthesis will be reduced through a reduction factor, and will completely stop as the cohort reaches the “longevity” core species parameter for its species. This is how tree cohort die of old age in PnET-Succession : by becoming progressively unable to sustain their maintenance costs through photosynthesis.

As such, there are two targets that are being calibrated here : 1) the age where photosynthesis will start declining because of age, and 2) the age where the photosynthesis reduction factor will become so low that the cohort will die by lack of ressources. These two targets are shown on the following figure :

This is a comment; I had to find a way to resize the image in Jupyter lab.
That makes for a snippet a code that is more complex than I'd want. See https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1885.
The first target (initiation of decline) is defined in PnET with the parameter

children.title: Reduction factor of photosynthesis as the age of an age cohort increases.

children.value: , which controls when the photosynthesis reduction factor fAge starts declining, and how fast.
The second target (age of cohort death) is influenced by the start of the decline (first target), but also  with the “longevity” parameter given in the “Core” species parameter file (e.g.

). When the age of the cohort reaches the “longevity” parameter, the photosynthesis reduction factor fAge will reach 0 (no more photosynthesis takes place). Of course, the cohort will most likely die before it reaches 0 because its photosynthesis will have been too low for too much time, which will have depleted its resources before fAge reaches 0. As such, we will try to input a “longevity” parameter value in the species parameter file that will kill the cohort at the age we want (e.g. that correspond to empirical data) in interaction with your first target. Note again that the cohort will very likely die

As to where we can get estimates of these two targets (initiation of decline and age of cohort death) for our species, this gets a bit complex. I used (see Notebook 4) the methodology of Brian Sturtevant’s team, which define the initiation of decline as the “Typical longevity” of a tree species as published by
, and the age of death of the “Maximal longevity” available in the same study. However, other sources are possible; see Notebook 4 for more.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

resultDict = {}

for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    resultDict[speciesCode] = calibrate_senescence(
        species = speciesCode,
        typical_mortality = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Longevity"], # TYPMORT from Loehle
        maximum_mortality = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Maximum longevity"], # MAXMORT from Loehle
        target_peak_biomass = SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets[speciesCode]["Peak_Biomass"],
        DictOfBounds = json.load(open("./SpeciesParametersSets/SpeciesParameterBounds.json")),
        dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
        dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
        tolerance=3,
        max_iterations=50,
        simulation_duration=500,
        climate="mild",
        soil="SILO")


# Re-writing parameter dicts
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.3/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    for parameter in ["PsnAgeRed", "TOWood", "TORoot"]:
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][parameter] = resultDict[species][parameter]
    dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters[species]["Longevity"] = str(resultDict[species]["Longevity"])
if "PsnAgeRed" in dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters.keys(): del dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters["PsnAgeRed"]

if os.path.exists("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4"):
    shutil.rmtree("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4")
os.mkdir("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4")

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

children.value:
from functionsForCalibration import *
import json

with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)

with open('./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets.json', 'r') as file: SpeciesTolerancesAndTargets = json.load(file)

listOfGrowthPnETCheckups = list()
listOfGrowthCurvesGAMS = list()

# For each species, we run some simple simulations with a) the initial set of parameters (uncalibrated) and b) more recent set so far
# We save the resulting growth curves into .csv files
# Then, we plot these growth curves next to each other.
# We will also plot the growth curves that came from the NFI data.
for speciesCode in speciesDict:

    calibrationSimulationMonoculturemanawan(duration = 300,
                                            climate = "mild",
                                            soil = "SILO",
                                            speciesToSimulate = speciesCode,
                                            dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
                                            plotResults = False,
                                           saveGrowthCurvePath = "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET_initial.csv")
    
    calibrationSimulationMonoculturemanawan(duration = 300,
                                            climate = "mild",
                                            soil = "SILO",
                                            speciesToSimulate = speciesCode,
                                            dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json')),
                                            dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Calibrated_SubSubPhase1.4/InitialGenericParameters.json')),
                                            plotResults = False,
                                           saveGrowthCurvePath = "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET.csv")

    plot_growth_curves("./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/" + str(speciesDict[speciesCode]) + "_gam_predictions.csv",
                       "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET.csv",
                      speciesCode,
                      "./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET_initial.csv")

    # We put the path of the NFI curves and the final growth curves in lists for the last plot where we will put all of the curves.
    listOfGrowthCurvesGAMS.append("./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/" + str(speciesDict[speciesCode]) + "_gam_predictions.csv")
    listOfGrowthPnETCheckups.append("./SpeciesParametersSets/Checkup_Step1/" + str(speciesCode) + "_Growth_PnET.csv")

# We then use another custom function to plot all of the growth curves of the tree species next to each other.
plot_multispecies_growth_curves(
    listOfGrowthCurvesGAMS,
    listOfGrowthPnETCheckups,
    list(speciesDict.keys())

)
